content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322-5662 
Robert Calfee 
995 Wing Place 
Stanford, CA 
94305 
Dear Mr. Calfee: 
Administration 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
321-5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
October 11, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice, Our No. A-83-227 
You have written requesting our advice with regard to your 
possible election to and service on the Palo Alto School Board. 
Because your wife is a kindergarten teacher in the district, you 
wish to know what potential conflict of interest disqualifi-
cation issues are raised by your service on the board, 
particularly vis a vis contract negotiations. 
First, we should 
point out that our advice is confined to the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (lithe Act")ll and does not cover other 
provisions of law which may, or may not apply. 
The conflict of interest provision of the Political Reform 
Act states as follows: 
No official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
II Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 
For other areas of the law you may wish to consult 
with the Office of the Attorney General, County Counsel, or a 
private attorney of your choice. 


Robert Clafee 
October 11, 1983 
Page 2 
Thus, the Act does not bar someone from holding office, it only 
prohibits someone with a financial interest from playing a role 
in those decisions which affect that interest. 
What constitutes 
a financial interest is set forth in Section 87103, as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
Since Section 82030(b) (2)"£/ excludes your wife's salary from 
being considered as income, there is no basis for a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform 
Act. 
A public official is permitted to participate in setting 
his own salary (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d) (3), copy 
enclosed) i for the same reasons he is likewise permitted to 
participate in setting his spouse's governmental salary. 
Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to 
call me at (916) 322-5901. 
REL:plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
y,L, 
- ~{L 
Robert E. Leidigh -
Counsel 
Legal Division 
/ 
/ ,,,-
II 
'£/ 
Section 82030(b) (2) excludes salary and wages fr0in a 
governmental entity. 


Calfee 
995 Wing PI. 
Stanford, CA 
94305

sections.question: Because your wife is a kindergarten teacher in the district, you wish to know what potential conflict of interest disqualification issues are raised by your service on the board, particularly vis a vis contract negotiations.
sections.conclusion: Since Section 82030(b)(2) excludes your wife's salary from being considered as income, there is no basis for a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. A public official is permitted to participate in setting his own salary for the same reasons he is likewise permitted to participate in setting his spouse's governmental salary.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a school board candidate have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if his wife is a kindergarten teacher in the district, particularly regarding contract negotiations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes there is no disqualifying conflict of interest because spousal government salaries are excluded from the definition of financial interest under the Political Reform Act, and public officials may participate in setting their spouse's governmental salary.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter with explicit question and conclusion sections. Document properly identifies the legal issue, applicable statute (Section 87100 and 87103), and provides reasoned analysis with statutory citations. Minor note: 'Clafee' misspelling appears on page 2 header but 'Calfee' is correct elsewhere.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Because your wife is a kindergarten teacher in the district, you wish to know what potential conflict of interest disqualification issues are raised by your service on the board, particularly vis a vis contract negotiations.

CONCLUSION: Since Section 82030(b)(2) excludes your wife's salary from being considered as income, there is no basis for a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. A public official is permitted to participate in setting his own salary for the same reasons he is likewise permitted to participate in setting his spouse's governmental salary.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Robert Calfee that his election to the Palo Alto School Board would not create a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act despite his wife being a kindergarten teacher in the district, as spousal government salaries are statutorily excluded from the definition of financial interest.
